texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The implementation of seawater desalination, a key strategy to secure fresh water supply in arid and coastal regions, is greatly thwarted by its high cost relating to high energy and equipment cost associated with pressure and the high system or membrane fouling associated with the bi-valence ions in the sea water. Nanofiltration (NF), a lower pressure membrane process with high rejection for divalent ions, had been identified as a key component to reduce the costs relating to both pressure and fouling in the desalination process. Lower cost seawater desalination processes had been developed by integrating NF with various types of desalination technologies including reverse osmosis (RO), forward osmosis (FO), electrodialysis (ED), multistage flash (MSF), multieffect distillation (MED), membrane distillation (MD) and ion exchange (IX). In this paper, after comparing the performance of currently available NF membranes, we attempt to review the recent progresses made in the development of lower cost seawater desalination processes using NF technologies and provide future perspectives for NF technologies.

texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Only about 0.5% of the overall global water is available as fresh water while seawater accounts for about 97% of them. Approximately 41% of the world population live in the arid regions, thus, fresh water shortage is becoming a worldwide problem [1 -2]. Seawater desalination has become an important way to secure freshwater supply for lots of countries, such as the Gulf States, Spain and China [3 -4], whose capacity is expected to reach about 100 million cubic meters per day in 2015 [5].
texts.text: Only about 0.5% of the overall global water is available as fresh water while seawater accounts for about 97% of them. Approximately 41% of the world population live in the arid regions, thus, fresh water shortage is becoming a worldwide problem [1 -2]. Seawater desalination has become an important way to secure freshwater supply for lots of countries, such as the Gulf States, Spain and China [3 -4], whose capacity is expected to reach about 100 million cubic meters per day in 2015 [5].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Thermal and membrane based processes have been developed for sea water desalination, including multistage flash (MSF), multieffect distillation (MED) and reverse osmosis (RO) [6 -7]. Recently, the capacity of membrane based desalination processes (mainly RO) had surpassed thermal based processes (mainly MSF and MED), because of their lower operating and investment costs [5 -10]. In the MSF and MED processes, plants usually cannot operate at more effective Top Brine Temperature (TBT) because of reactor scaling [15 -16]. Although, great efforts have been conducted to improve the efficiency and reduce the energy consumption of desalination technology [2], the worldwide implementation of sea water desalination is still thwarted by its high operating costs associated with high system pressure and bivalent ionic fouling [11 -16].

Recently, the capacity of membrane based desalination processes (mainly RO) had surpassed thermal based processes (mainly MSF and MED), because of their lower operating and investment costs [5 -10]. In the MSF and MED processes, plants usually cannot operate at more effective Top Brine Temperature (TBT) because of reactor scaling [15 -16]. Although, great efforts have been conducted to improve the efficiency and reduce the energy consumption of desalination technology [2], the worldwide implementation of sea water desalination is still thwarted by its high operating costs associated with high system pressure and bivalent ionic fouling [11 -16].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Nanofiltration (NF), an effective pressure-driven membrane process, has the pore size and cut off ability between RO and ultra-filtration [18 -20]. Compared with RO, it operates not only under lower operation pressures, higher water fluxes, and lower investment, but also with high rejection rates for scale formation bivalent ions, especially anions [21 -23]. With these characters, it is gaining its yards quickly in the seawater desalination field [25]. This paper compared the performance of currently available NF, reviewed the progresses made in the uses of NF in developing lower cost seawater desalination processes, and attempted to provide future perspectives for NF techniques in sea water desalination.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Performance of commercially available NF membranes
texts.text: 2. Performance of commercially available NF membranes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Due to the unique separation ability, NF technology has been developed for removing scaling ions and low-molecular-weight organics as well as part of the NaCl from seawater [26,27]. A lot of efforts have been devoted to evaluate their performance under different application conditions. Some of the currently available data for the commercial NF membrane are listed in Table 1 for comparison. It is obvious that the ionic rejection ratios of NF membranes varied greatly among the brands. Hilal et al. [28] investigated the desalination performance of three kinds of nanofiltration membranes NF90, NF270 (Dow Filmtec), and N30F (MICRODYN-NADIR) and found that the flux and salt rejection rate of NF90 and NF270 agreed well to the Spigler -Kedem model. It was suggested that NF90 might be the best choice for NaCl removal, having the highest rejection but medium permeates flux. Llenas et al. [29] evaluated the performance of six commercial NF membranes, NF270, NF200, NF90 (Dow Filmtec), K-SR2 (Koch), ESNA1-LF2 (Hydranautics), and NF99HF (Alfa Laval) and found all of them showed high rejection to divalent ions, especially sulfate ions. They suggested that NF270, K-SR2 and NF99HF membranes were more suitable for pretreatment in RO desalination. Song et al. [30] suggested that ESNA3 membrane was quite suitable for softening sea water while Pontié et al. [31 -32] found that the use of NF200 membrane could bear potential system flaws under the conditions of high pressure and high salt concentration. Llenas et al. [33] also suggested that NF270, K-SR2 and NF99HF be suitable for removing scaling ions in seawater.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Alot of detailed studies were carried out for hardness ion concentration polarization, membrane scaling and organic fouling on the surface of NF membranes. It was found that organic fouling mostly occurred on the lead elements while inorganic scaling mostly occurred on the last elements [34]. A1-Amoudi et al. [35] successfully restored NF permeate flux after scaling using SDS (sodium dodecyl sulfate) cleaning and PH swinging methods. Song et al. [36] observed that ion rejection and permeate flux of ESNA3 declined with time due to inorganic fouling on the surface of NF membranes, and found that CaSO4 scaling could form but CaCO3 could not at high recovery rate. By scale inhibitor addition and pH adjustment, NF water recovery rates reached 60% without CaSO4 scaling [37 -38]. NF technology was an effective treatment process for removing most of divalent ions and reducing the TDS of seawater. Their stronger separation power for organics provides better protection for the safe operation of the next process.

texts.orig: 3. Integration of NF in membrane based desalination processes
texts.text: 3. Integration of NF in membrane based desalination processes

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Dual stage NF desalination process
texts.text: 3.1. Dual stage NF desalination process

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Asymmetric NF membranes with tight polyamide separating layer (such as NF90 and ESNA1) possessed high rejection ratios for both monovalent and bivalent ions. Based on this, Vuong [39 -40] developed two stage NF -NF seawater desalination systems, effectively removing ions from seawater with 20% to 30% lower energy cost than conventional one-stage RO. It was reported that a fully operational NF -NF process has been established in a facility in Long Beach, United States with a daily water production of 1135 m 3 [41]. Harrison et al. [42] found that the average permeate TDS was less than 400 mg/L when the high rejection NF-90 was used in both stages. Altaee et al. [43] studied the performance of NF -NF desalination process with ROSA simulation software. At 35,000 mg/L feed salinity, the pressure of the first and second stages was 37 bar and 19 bar, and the recovery was 59% and 67%, respectively. The permeate TDS and energy consumption of the NF90 -NF90 system was 254 mg/L and 3.35 kWh/m 3 , respectively. Liu et al. [44] found that the operation pressure had the greatest effects on flux and TDS of permeate water in the NF90 -NF90 system. In consideration of water quality and energy consumption, the dual-stage NF seawater desalination process was a promising seawater desalination technology.

texts.orig: 3.2. Integrated NF and RO desalination processes
texts.text: 3.2. Integrated NF and RO desalination processes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Integrating NF with RO desalination process may increases the complexity and cost of desalination plant. However, NF pretreatment had shown effectiveness in removing divalent ions and reducing osmotic pressure from the RO feed water, and integrated NF with RO could join the advantages from both kinds of membranes [45 -46]. Hassan et al. [27,48] and Uhlinger [47] proposed the NF -RO low cost seawater desalination process, and showed that, at the low pressure of only 22 bar, the Ca 2+ , Mg 2+ , SO4 2 -, HCO3 -and total hardness rejection of NF were 89.4%, 94.0%, 97.8%, 96.6% and 93.3%, respectively, and the rejection rate of monovalent ions (Cl -, Na + ) was 40.3%, achieving about 27% reduction in the net water production cost from one-stage SWRO.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Chen et al. [55 -56] proposed an integrated RO -NF desalination process to increase the overall water recovery. In the process, the concentrated water produced by the RO process was further desalted by NF process, where the permeate water was feedback to RO. Using SW30 (Dow Filmtec) 4-inch RO membrane elements and 2-inch NF membrane elements, at 71% water recovery, the water production cost was 0.57 $/m 3 , 20.06% lower than single stage RO process [57]. Compared with the two stage RO process, RO -NF process saved more investment costs and electric energy [58 -59].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. Integrated NF and FO desalination processes
texts.text: 3.3. Integrated NF and FO desalination processes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: FO is a natural process in which water was driven through a semipermeable membrane by an osmotic pressure gradient. Majority of the studies of FO membranes have been focused on either power generation or water production [60,62 -63,67]. FO has been proposed for seawater desalination since the 1970s [64]. In a FO -ROdesalination system, the role of FO pass was drawing water from seawater, and the RO produced freshwater by concentrating the diluted FO draw solution [65 -66]. Tan et al. [68] further proposed a FO -NF -NF system as shown in Fig. 1. Similar to the FO -RO system, water was first driven from seawater to draw solution through the FO membrane, and then the two-stage NF process removed salt from diluted draw solution to form fresh water. Seven draw solutes (NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, MgSO4, Na2SO4 and C6H12O6) were tested, of which, NaCl and KCl showed the highest water flux of 25 L m -2 h -1 . The rejection of FO membrane for the seven draw solutes (according to the above order) were 99.47%, 99.48%, 99.98%, 99.97%, 99.99%, 99.97% and 99.86%, respectively. For RO -NF -NF process, Na2SO4 was the most appropriate draw solute for its high single NF rejection of above 90%. The expected dual stage rejection in the process was 99.92% [69].

texts.orig: 3.4. Integrated NF and ED desalination processes
texts.text: 3.4. Integrated NF and ED desalination processes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ED technology also has potential to concentrate the brine of RO, NF -RO and NF -RO -MED processes to become suitable feed for membrane electrolysis for producing alkali [71 -74]. For thermal based seawater desalination processes, ED -MSF -Crystallization has become effective low cost multifunction desalination -salt production processes [70]. As discussed before, dual-stage seawater NF process showed poor capability in rejecting boron [75 -76]. Turek et al. [77] found that ED process could remove the boron to the acceptable level (0.5 mg/L) from the permeate water of two-stage NF at the cost of about 0.098 $/m 3 and the energy consumption of about 0.245 kWh/m 3 . Better boron removal occurred at higher pH value. The boron concentration of permeate was reduced from 2.4 mg/L to 0.5 mg/L when the ED process was running for 30 min at 110 C/L and pH value of 12.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: for a long time while MSF -RO plants had even higher water recovery rates [78 -81]. However, sulfate scaling has limited their TBT (Top Brine Temperature) and flashing range, impacting unit capital and freshwater cost [82]. Hassan et al. [43,83 -84] proposed a NF -RO -MSF desalination process as shown in Fig. 2, in which NF membranes were used to remove scale forming ions from seawater, allowing higher TBT operation of the MSF processes. Not only was the water productivity improved but also the service life of cascade staging of MSF distillers was extended. However, the feed water had to be heated process in winter [85 -88]. In this process, Hamed et al. [89] showed that the NF process could reject 82.8% of the hardness ions and 26.5% of the TDS at the recovery rate of 64 -69%. The TBT and water conversion ratio of MSF unit could reach the high temperature of 130 -160 °C. The combination of NF -MSF and NF -RO -MSF with power plant could achieve less capital costs, high plant availability and better water production, when the local low cost electricity from the power plant applied to the NF and RO membrane processes, and the waste heat could be used by the MSF process.
texts.text: for a long time while MSF -RO plants had even higher water recovery rates [78 -81]. However, sulfate scaling has limited their TBT (Top Brine Temperature) and flashing range, impacting unit capital and freshwater cost [82]. Hassan et al. [43,83 -84] proposed a NF -RO -MSF desalination process as shown in Fig. 2, in which NF membranes were used to remove scale forming ions from seawater, allowing higher TBT operation of the MSF processes. Not only was the water productivity improved but also the service life of cascade staging of MSF distillers was extended. However, the feed water had to be heated process in winter [85 -88]. In this process, Hamed et al. [89] showed that the NF process could reject 82.8% of the hardness ions and 26.5% of the TDS at the recovery rate of 64 -69%. The TBT and water conversion ratio of MSF unit could reach the high temperature of 130 -160 °C. The combination of NF -MSF and NF -RO -MSF with power plant could achieve less capital costs, high plant availability and better water production, when the local low cost electricity from the power plant applied to the NF and RO membrane processes, and the waste heat could be used by the MSF process.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mabrouk et al. [90 -91] evaluated the desalination performance of CSP -NF -MSF -DBM (concentrated solar power -NF -MSF -deaeration and brine mix) process. When about 90% of sulfate and calcium ions were rejected by NF, the TBT of the NF -MSF -DBMplant could be raised to 100 -130 °C to achieve a gain output ratio (GOR) of 15 -16, doubled from the existing multistage flash-brine recirculation (MSF-BR) plant. Fresh water production was increased by 19% at 14% less cost. When the discharged concentrated brine was further used as feed for the crystallization, NF -MSF -Crystallization (NF -MSF -Cr) or NF -RO -MSF -Crystallization (NF -RO -MSF -Cr) systems produced not only fresh water but also high quality salt crystals [92]. Their water production costs were about 0.71 $/m 3 and 0.43 $/m 3 . Subtracting the salt price of 30 $/ton [93], the cost of fresh water production could be further lowered to 0.37 $/m 3 [94].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. Integrated NF and MED desalination processes
texts.text: 4.2. Integrated NF and MED desalination processes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The MED processes have been developed based on high efficient heat transport from condensing steam of the previous evaporator to saline water of the next evaporator in a series of stages or effects to have high desalination efficiency [95 -97]. Their costs may be further reduced using the solar energy or waste heat of power plant [98 -99]. Integrated NF -MED desalination processes are more effective energy saving processes for fresh water production [100]. With NF pretreatment, its operating TBT may be increased from 65 °C to 125 °C without the danger of scaling [86,88]. Turek et al. [101] evaluated the performance of three NF membranes, NF200 and NF270, TFC-SR2 (Koch), in NF -RO -MED -Crystallization (NF -RO -MED -Cr) desalination systems and found that the retention coefficient of TFC-SR2 was the highest. The rejection rates for hardness ions and Cl -for the optimized systems were 87.9% and 23.7% at the pressure of 14 bar, respectively. Similar to other membrane -thermal integrating systems, the NF in these integrated processes could enhance the recovery ratio and decrease the water production costs of MED. The recovery and water production cost of the integrated system were 78.2% and 0.5 $/m 3 , respectively.

texts.orig: 4.3. Integrated NF and MD/MC desalination processes
texts.text: 4.3. Integrated NF and MD/MC desalination processes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane distillation (MD) is attractive in seawater desalination for its low cost, high rejection (100%, theoretical), low operating temperature and pressure, and energy saving perspectives [102 -106], whose production cost of MD could be further reduced by using solar energy and waste lower grade heat sources [107]. Drioli et al. [108 -109] used NF and membrane contactor units as pretreatment for RO -MDdesalination processes to form a hybrid NF -RO -MSF seawater desalination system as shown in Fig. 3. NF unit helped solve the scaling problemof MDbyremovinghardnessions[110]. The overallrecovery of the hybrid desalination system was 76.2%, the TDS of production water was 310 ppm and the production cost was 0.92 $/m 3 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane crystallization (MC) combined the application of membrane process with crystallization technology [111], producing freshwater as well as salt crystals from seawater desalination [112]. The technology is very attractive in eliminating the environmental concern from the discharge of harmful concentrated water from membrane based desalination processes [113]. NF -RO -MC process is a very efficient desalination technology, in which the NF stage removed about 90% of scaling ions and 27.6% of TDS when the recovery of the RO and MCwas50%and 100%, respectively. Moreover, the quality of pure crystals product was enhanced, and the cost of product water was less than that of NF and RO [114 -115].

texts.orig: Drioli et al. [116 -118] integrated the MF -NF -ROsystem with membrane distillation/crystallization (MD/MC) units to achieve the high water recovery rates of 92.8%. By using available waste thermal energy and brine pressure exchanger system, the energy consumption of the NF -RO -MC process (NF -RO and MC both on NF and RO retentate) and NF -RO -MD/MC process (NF -RO, MC on NF retentate and MD on RO retentate) were reduced to 1.54 -1.61 kWh/m 3 at the fresh water production costs of about 0.51 $/m 3 . In consideration of the gain from the salt sale (NaCl, Na2CO3, CaCO3, MgSO4·7H2O), the net fresh water unit costs of the processes may be further lowered to negative numbers of -0.71 $/m 3 and -0.36 $/m 3 , respectively.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. Integrated NF and ion exchange processes
texts.text: 5. Integrated NF and ion exchange processes

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Boron is harmful to the human body, and could not be effectively removed by SWRO membrane process. IX (ion exchange) is an effective method to remove ions [119], and had been applied in membrane seawater desalination for a long time, which is a very important technology for effective removal of boron. Therefore, RO-IXR treatment is considered as a useful method to remove boron from permeates from RO seawater desalination systems [120 -122]. Sarkar et al. [123 -126] further proposed the ion exchange -nanofiltration (HIX -NF) desalination process as shown in Fig. 4. Firstly, polymeric ion exchange resins exchanged the monovalent ions in seawater to divalent ions which could then be effectively removed by NF processes. In the operation, NF could reject more than 98% of the Na2SO4 under the overall energy consumption of 0.9 kWh/m 3 and 50% reduction from RO processes. HIX -NF desalination process made a great advance in saving energy, but the costs and working life of ion exchange resin are still limiting its applications.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Integrating with NF was, and will be a key strategy to lower the costs of current desalination systems. Some of the key performance factors of the developed desalination processes using NF are summarized in Table 2 for comparison.

texts.orig: In consideration of water quality and energy consumption, the dualstage NF seawater desalination process using high rejection NF was a promising lower cost desalination technology for the future. Using high cut off asymmetric polyimide based NF (such as NF90), and integrating with ED process could improve the product water quality and solve the boron problem in the systems.

texts.orig: As a mature technique, NF -RO process using NF270, K-SR2 and NF99HF, showed significantly reduced water production costs and RO membrane fouling. NF -SWRO plant could save product water costs by 30%. Additional research should be carried out to reduce NF membrane fouling and further enhance its water flux under high ion rejection. In consideration of the FO -NF -NF process, Na2SO4 was the most appropriate draw solute. The HIX -NFsystemcouldsave50%powerconsumption of that of RO and exhibit larger permeate flux. Additional research relating to FO -NF -NF and HIX -NF technology is needed in designing lower cost high performance FO membranes and ion exchange resins.
